text: Welcome back to Practical Systems. Today we're talking about security design, and I'm joined by Priya, principal SRE at a payments platform. Priya, thanks for being here.
Thanks, Ethan. Great to be back.

Yeah, exactly. And it ties into what we're covering today.
I used to argue for a single central security team. I changed my mind after the 2024 webhook incident — the embedded model gets faster fixes and better signal.
That's a real shift. Worth pulling on.
What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? Where do reliability and security design intersect?

Yeah, so — Secrets handling improves when tokens are short-lived, scoped narrowly, and rotated with automation. We've seen this play out at teams using Notion. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're actually making.
Where do most teams get it wrong? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?

Let me give you the version I actually believe. Threat modeling can be lightweight: identify assets, entry points, trust boundaries, and likely abuse cases. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
Take me through the decision. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?

That maps to what I've seen.
I've seen this with teams running Notion and teams running everything in-house. What used to feel like an exception is honestly the median case now.
What does the failure mode look like? Where do people most often confuse correlation with causation in this area?

I'd put it this way: Authn/authz separation matters more than people think — most breaches I've seen confused who someone is with what they can do. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
How do you know when you've gotten it right? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?

Let me give you the version I actually believe. Security incidents are reliability incidents — Datadog can show you the spike, but only the on-call structure makes it survivable. We've seen this play out at teams using Linear. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
I want to dig into that. What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?

Honestly, I used to think security teams should own all the security work. After three incidents I revised that — embedded ownership beats a central gatekeeper every time. The exception proves the rule — there are edge cases, but they're rarer than people assume.
Where does that break down?
I've seen this with teams running Sentry and teams running everything in-house. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
